question_text: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}{/tex} . Is {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|{/tex} ? Are the vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} equal?
question_html: Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}{/tex}. Is {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|{/tex}? Are the vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} equal?
solution_text: We have {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} So, {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|{/tex} . But, the two vectors are not equal since their corresponding components are distinct.
solution_html:
We have {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} and {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} 
So, {tex}|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|{/tex}. But, the two vectors are not equal since their corresponding components are distinct.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
question_html: Find unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
solution_text: The unit vector in the direction of a vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} is given by {tex}\hat{a}=\frac{1}{|\vec{a}|} \vec{a}{/tex} Now {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} Therefore {tex}\hat{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})=\frac{2}{\sqrt{14}} \hat{i}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
The unit vector in the direction of a vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} is given by {tex}\hat{a}=\frac{1}{|\vec{a}|} \vec{a}{/tex} 
Now {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} 
Therefore {tex}\hat{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})=\frac{2}{\sqrt{14}} \hat{i}+\frac{3}{\sqrt{14}} \hat{j}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}} \hat{k}{/tex}

question_text: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j{/tex} that has magnitude 7 units.
question_html: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i - 2\hat j{/tex} that has magnitude 7 units.
solution_text: {tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}{/tex} Vector having magnitude equal to 7 and in the direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex} is {tex}7\hat a = 7\left( {\frac{{\hat i - 2\hat j}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{7}{{\sqrt 5 }}\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right)}}{{\sqrt 5 }}{/tex}

Vector having magnitude equal to 7 and in the direction of {tex}\vec a{/tex}is

{tex}7\hat a = 7\left( {\frac{{\hat i - 2\hat j}}{{\sqrt 5 }}} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{7}{{\sqrt 5 }}\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the unit vector in the direction of the sum of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} .
question_html: Find the unit vector in the direction of the sum of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex} {tex} = \left( {2\hat i + 2\hat j - 5\hat k} \right) + \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = 4\hat i + 3\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {16 + 9 + 4} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex} The required unit vector is {tex}\hat c = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4\vec i + 3\vec j - 2\vec k}}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{4\hat i + 3\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{4}{{\sqrt {29} }}\hat i + \frac{3}{{\sqrt {29} }}\hat j - \frac{2}{{\sqrt {29} }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex}
{tex} = \left( {2\hat i + 2\hat j - 5\hat k} \right) + \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 4\hat i + 3\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {16 + 9 + 4} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex}
The required unit vector is
{tex}\hat c = \frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{4\vec i + 3\vec j - 2\vec k}}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{4\hat i + 3\hat j - 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{4}{{\sqrt {29} }}\hat i + \frac{3}{{\sqrt {29} }}\hat j - \frac{2}{{\sqrt {29} }}\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find the vector joining the points P(2, 3, 0) and Q(-1, -2, -4) directed from P to Q.
question_html: Find the vector joining the points P(2, 3, 0) and Q(-1, -2, -4) directed from P to Q.
solution_text: Since the vector is to be directed from P to Q, clearly P is the initial point and Q is the terminal point. So, the required vector joining P and Q is the vector {tex}\vec {PQ}{/tex} , given by {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(-1-2) \hat{i}+(-2-3) \hat{j}+(-4-0) \hat{k}{/tex} i.e. {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=-3 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Consider two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\vec{OP}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{OQ}=\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} . Find the position vector (internally) of a point R which divides the line joining P and Q in the ratio 2 : 1.
question_html: Consider two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\vec{OP}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{OQ}=\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex}. Find the position vector (internally) of a point R which divides the line joining P and Q in the ratio 2 : 1.
solution_text: The position vector of the point R dividing the join of P and Q internally in the ratio 2 : 1 is {tex}\vec{OR}=\frac{2(\vec{a}+\vec{b})+(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2+1}=\frac{5 \vec{a}}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
The position vector of the point R dividing the join of P and Q internally in the ratio 2 : 1 is
{tex}\vec{OR}=\frac{2(\vec{a}+\vec{b})+(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2+1}=\frac{5 \vec{a}}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Consider two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\vec{OP}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{OQ}=\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} . Find the position vector (externally) of a point R which divides the line joining P and Q in the ratio 2 : 1.
question_html:
Consider two points P and Q with position vectors {tex}\vec{OP}=3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{OQ}=\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex}.
Find the position vector (externally) of a point R which divides the line joining P and Q in the ratio 2 : 1.
solution_text: The position vector of the point R dividing the join of P and Q externally in the ratio 2 : 1 is {tex}\vec{OR}=\frac{2(\vec{a}+\vec{b})-(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2-1}=4 \vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
The position vector of the point R dividing the join of P and Q externally in the ratio 2 : 1 is
{tex}\vec{OR}=\frac{2(\vec{a}+\vec{b})-(3 \vec{a}-2 \vec{b})}{2-1}=4 \vec{b}-\vec{a}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the points {tex}A\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right),B\left( {\hat i - 3\hat j - 5\hat k} \right),C\left( {3\hat i - 4\hat j - 4\hat k} \right){/tex} are the vertices of a right angled triangle.
question_html: Show that the points {tex}A\left( {2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right),B\left( {\hat i - 3\hat j - 5\hat k} \right),C\left( {3\hat i - 4\hat j - 4\hat k} \right){/tex} are the vertices of a right angled triangle.
solution_text: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = - \hat i - 2\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec{BC}=2\vec i-\vec j+\vec k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {CA} = - \hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} ={1^2+2^2+6^2}= 41{/tex} {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2 = 2^2+1^2+1^2=6{/tex} {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2 = 1^2+3^2+5^2=35{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} = {\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2} + {\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2}{/tex} Hence, the {tex}\Delta {/tex} is a right angled triangle.
solution_html:
{tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = - \hat i - 2\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec{BC}=2\vec i-\vec j+\vec k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow {CA} = - \hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} ={1^2+2^2+6^2}= 41{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2 = 2^2+1^2+1^2=6{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2 = 1^2+3^2+5^2=35{/tex}
{tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} = {\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2} + {\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2}{/tex}
Hence, the {tex}\Delta {/tex} is a right angled triangle.

question_text: Find the angle between two vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} with magnitudes 1 and 2 respectively and when {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1{/tex} .
question_html: Find the angle between two vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} with magnitudes 1 and 2 respectively and when {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1{/tex}.
solution_text: Given {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1,|\vec{a}|=1{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} . We have {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \| \vec{b}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=1,|\vec{a}|=1{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|=2{/tex}. We have
{tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a} \| \vec{b}|}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
question_html: Find angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
solution_text: The angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex} Now {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=1-1-1=-1{/tex} Therefore, we have {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-1}{3}{/tex} hence the required angle is {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
The angle {tex}\theta{/tex} between two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by 
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}{/tex} 
Now {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=1-1-1=-1{/tex} 
Therefore, we have {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-1}{3}{/tex} 
hence the required angle is {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}=5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} , then show that the vectors {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} are perpendicular.
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}=5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex}, then show that the vectors {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} are perpendicular.
solution_text: We know that , two non-zero vectors are perpendicular if their scalar product is zero. According to the Question , {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=(5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})=6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=(5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})=4 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} So , Dot Product of two vectors : {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b})=(6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}) \cdot(4 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=24-8-16=0{/tex} Hence, {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex} are perpendicular vectors.
solution_html:
We know that , two non-zero vectors are perpendicular if their scalar product is zero.
According to the Question , 

 {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=(5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})=6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} 
and  {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}=(5 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})-(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})=4 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} 
So , Dot Product of two vectors :

 {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b})=(6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}) \cdot(4 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=24-8-16=0{/tex} 
Hence,  {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-\vec{b}{/tex}  are perpendicular vectors.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the projection of the vector {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} on the vector {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the projection of the vector {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} on the vector {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}.
solution_text: The projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on the vector {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})=\frac{(2 \times 1+3 \times 2+2 \times 1)}{\sqrt{(1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(1)^{2}}}=\frac{10}{\sqrt{6}}=\frac{5}{3} \sqrt{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
The projection of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} on the vector {tex}\vec b{/tex} is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{|\vec{b}|}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})=\frac{(2 \times 1+3 \times 2+2 \times 1)}{\sqrt{(1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(1)^{2}}}=\frac{10}{\sqrt{6}}=\frac{5}{3} \sqrt{6}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex} , if two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=2,|\vec{b}|=3{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=4{/tex} .
question_html: Find {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|{/tex}, if two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=2,|\vec{b}|=3{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=4{/tex}.
solution_text: We have {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}-\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}-\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}-2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} = {tex}(2)^{2}-2(4)+(3)^{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sqrt{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|^{2}=(\vec{a}-\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} 
= {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}-\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}-\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} 
= {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}-2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}(2)^{2}-2(4)+(3)^{2}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}|\vec{a}-\vec{b}|=\sqrt{5}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If {tex}{\vec a}{/tex} is a unit vector and {tex}(\vec{x}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{x}+\vec{a})=8{/tex} , then find {tex}\left| {\vec x} \right|{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}{\vec a}{/tex} is a unit vector and {tex}(\vec{x}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{x}+\vec{a})=8{/tex}, then find {tex}\left| {\vec x} \right|{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 1{/tex} {tex}\left( {\vec x - \vec a} \right).\left( {\vec x + \vec a} \right) = 8{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\vec x} \right|^2} - |\vec a|^2 = 8{/tex} {tex}|\vec x|^2-1=8{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\vec x} \right|^2} = 9{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec x} \right| = 3{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 1{/tex}
{tex}\left( {\vec x - \vec a} \right).\left( {\vec x + \vec a} \right) = 8{/tex}
{tex}{\left| {\vec x} \right|^2} - |\vec a|^2 = 8{/tex}
{tex}|\vec x|^2-1=8{/tex}
{tex}{\left| {\vec x} \right|^2} = 9{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\vec x} \right| = 3{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that for any two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} , we always have {tex}|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} (CauchySchwartz inequality).
question_html: Prove that for any two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex}, we always have {tex}|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} (CauchySchwartz inequality).
solution_text: The inequality holds trivially when either {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex} . Actually, in such a situation we have {tex}|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|=0=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex} . So, let us assume that {tex}|\vec{a}| \neq 0 \neq|\vec{b}|{/tex} . Then, we have {tex}\frac{|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=|\cos \theta| \leq 1{/tex} Therefore {tex}|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}
solution_html:
The inequality holds trivially when either {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex}. Actually, in such a situation we have {tex}|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|=0=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}. So, let us assume that {tex}|\vec{a}| \neq 0 \neq|\vec{b}|{/tex}.
Then, we have {tex}\frac{|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}=|\cos \theta| \leq 1{/tex} 
Therefore {tex}|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that for any two vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} , we always have {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|{/tex} (triangle inequality).
question_html: Show that for any two vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} , we always have {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|{/tex} (triangle inequality).
solution_text: The inequality holds trivially in case either {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex} . So, let {tex}|\vec{a}| \neq \vec{0} \neq|\vec{b}|{/tex} . Then {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b})^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} (scalar product is commuatative) = {tex}\leq|\vec{a}|^{2}+2|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} (since {tex}x \leq|x|~ \forall x \in {R}{/tex} ) = {tex}\leq|\vec{a}|^{2}+2|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} (from Cauchy Schwartz Inequality) = {tex}(|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a}+\vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|{/tex}
solution_html:
The inequality holds trivially in case either {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex}.
So, let {tex}|\vec{a}| \neq \vec{0} \neq|\vec{b}|{/tex}. Then
{tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b})^{2}=(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} 
= {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} 
= {tex}|\vec{a}|^{2}+2 \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} (scalar product is commuatative)
={tex}\leq|\vec{a}|^{2}+2|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} (since {tex}x \leq|x|~ \forall x \in {R}{/tex})
={tex}\leq|\vec{a}|^{2}+2|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} (from Cauchy Schwartz Inequality) 
= {tex}(|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|)^{2}{/tex}  
{tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{a}+\vec{b}| \leq|\vec{a}|+|\vec{b}|{/tex}

question_text: Show that the points {tex}A ( - 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) , B ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex}C ( 7 \hat { i } - \hat { k } ){/tex} are collinear.
question_html: Show that the points  {tex}A ( - 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) , B ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex}C ( 7 \hat { i } - \hat { k } ){/tex} are collinear.
solution_text: According to the question , Given points are {tex}A ( - 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) , {/tex} {tex}B ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ),{/tex} and {tex}C ( 7 \hat { i} - \hat { k } ).{/tex} Consider, {tex}\vec { A B } = ( \hat {i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) - ( - 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat {j} + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\vec { B C } = ( { 7} \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 6 \hat {i} - 2 \hat {j} - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\vec { A C } = ( 7 \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) - ( - 2 \hat {i} + 3 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= 9 \hat {i } - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, consider, {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } = ( 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}+ ( 6 \hat { i } - 2 \hat {j } - 4 \hat { k } ) = 9 \hat { i} - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } = \vec { A C }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The points A, B and C are collinear.
solution_html:
According to the question ,
Given points are {tex}A ( - 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) , {/tex}

{tex}B ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ),{/tex} and 

{tex}C ( 7 \hat { i} - \hat { k } ).{/tex}
Consider, {tex}\vec { A B } = ( \hat {i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) - ( - 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat {j} + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex}{tex}= 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\vec { B C } = ( { 7} \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex}{tex}= 6 \hat {i} - 2 \hat {j} - 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}\vec { A C } = ( 7 \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) - ( - 2 \hat {i} + 3 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ){/tex}{tex}= 9 \hat {i } - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, consider, {tex}\vec { A B } + \vec { B C } = ( 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}{tex}+ ( 6 \hat { i } - 2 \hat {j } - 4 \hat { k } ) = 9 \hat { i} - 3 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } = \vec { A C }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The points A, B and C are collinear.

question_text: Find {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} if {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} if {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {1} & {3} \\ {3} & {5} & {-2} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}(-2-15)-(-4-9) \hat{j}+(10-3) \hat{k}=-17 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} Hence, {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(-17)^{2}+(13)^{2}+(7)^{2}}=\sqrt{507}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {2} & {1} & {3} \\ {3} & {5} & {-2} \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
= {tex}\hat{i}(-2-15)-(-4-9) \hat{j}+(10-3) \hat{k}=-17 \hat{i}+13 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{(-17)^{2}+(13)^{2}+(7)^{2}}=\sqrt{507}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right){/tex} , where {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k, \ \vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} .
question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right){/tex}, where {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k, \ \vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}\vec a+\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)+(\vec i+2\vec j+3\vec k)=2\vec i+3\vec j+4\vec k{/tex} {tex}\vec a-\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)-(\vec i+2\vec j+3 \vec k=-\vec j-2\vec k{/tex} A vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}(\vec a + \vec b){/tex} are {tex}(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} is given by {tex}(\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&4 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} Let {tex}\vec c = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {4 + 16 + 4} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {24} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt 6 {/tex} Required unit vector is {tex}\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a+\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)+(\vec i+2\vec j+3\vec k)=2\vec i+3\vec j+4\vec k{/tex}
{tex}\vec a-\vec b=(\vec i+\vec j+\vec k)-(\vec i+2\vec j+3 \vec k=-\vec j-2\vec k{/tex}
A vector which is perpendicular to both {tex}(\vec a + \vec b){/tex} are {tex}(\vec a - \vec b){/tex} is given by
{tex}(\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&4 \\ 0&{ - 1}&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec c = - 2\hat i + 4\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {4 + 16 + 4} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {24} {/tex}
{tex} = 2\sqrt 6 {/tex}
Required unit vector is
{tex}\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the area of a triangle having the points A(1, 1, 1), B(1, 2, 3) and C(2, 3, 1) as its vertices.
question_html: Find the area of a triangle having the points A(1, 1, 1), B(1, 2, 3) and C(2, 3, 1) as its vertices.
solution_text: We have {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}=\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AC}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}{/tex} . The area of the given triangle is {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{\mathrm{AB}} \times \vec{\mathrm{AC}}|{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} \times \vec{\mathrm{AC}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {0} & {1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{AB}} \times \vec{\mathrm{AC}}|=\sqrt{16+4+1}=\sqrt{21}{/tex} Thus, the required area is {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{21}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}=\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AC}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}{/tex}.
The area of the given triangle is {tex}\frac{1}{2}|\vec{\mathrm{AB}} \times \vec{\mathrm{AC}}|{/tex}
Now, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} \times \vec{\mathrm{AC}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {0} & {1} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{AB}} \times \vec{\mathrm{AC}}|=\sqrt{16+4+1}=\sqrt{21}{/tex} 
Thus, the required area is {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{21}{/tex}

question_text: Find the area of a parallelogram whose adjacent sides are given by the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
question_html: Find the area of a parallelogram whose adjacent sides are given by the vectors {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
solution_text: The area of a parallelogram with {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} as its adjacent sides is given by {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} . Now, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {3} & {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right|=5 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} Therefore {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{25+1+16}=\sqrt{42}{/tex} Hence, the required area is {tex}\sqrt{42}{/tex} .
solution_html:
The area of a parallelogram with {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} as its adjacent sides is given by {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}.
Now, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{i}} & {\hat{j}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {3} & {1} & {4} \\ {1} & {-1} & {1} \end{array}\right|=5 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} 
Therefore {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\sqrt{25+1+16}=\sqrt{42}{/tex} 
Hence, the required area is {tex}\sqrt{42}{/tex}.

question_text: Write all the unit vectors in XY-plane.
question_html: Write all the unit vectors in XY-plane.
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}{/tex} be a unit vector in XY-plane. Then, from the figure below, we have x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} (since {tex}|\vec r|{/tex} = 1). So, we may write the vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} as {tex}\vec{r}(=\vec{\mathrm{OP}})=\cos \theta ~ \hat{i}+\sin \theta ~\hat{j}{/tex} .........(i) Clearly, {tex}|\vec{r}|=\sqrt{\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}=1{/tex} Also, as {tex}\theta{/tex} varies from {tex}0 ~to ~2\pi{/tex} , the point P traces the circle x 2 + y 2 = 1 counterclockwise, and this covers all possible directions. i,e {tex}\vec{r}=\cos \theta \hat{i}+\sin \theta \hat{j} ; \quad \theta \in(0,2 \pi){/tex} , represents all the unit vectors in a plane.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{r}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}{/tex} be a unit vector in XY-plane.
Then, from the figure below, we have x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} (since {tex}|\vec r|{/tex} = 1).
So, we may write the vector {tex}\vec r{/tex} as
{tex}\vec{r}(=\vec{\mathrm{OP}})=\cos \theta ~ \hat{i}+\sin \theta ~\hat{j}{/tex} .........(i)
Clearly, {tex}|\vec{r}|=\sqrt{\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta}=1{/tex} 
 
Also, as {tex}\theta{/tex} varies from {tex}0 ~to ~2\pi{/tex}, the point P traces the circle x2 + y2 = 1 counterclockwise, and this covers all possible directions.
i,e {tex}\vec{r}=\cos \theta \hat{i}+\sin \theta \hat{j} ; \quad \theta \in(0,2 \pi){/tex}, represents all the unit vectors in a plane.

question_text: If {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}, 3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are the position vectors of points A, B, C and D respectively, then find the angle between {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} . Deduce that {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex} are collinear.
question_html: If {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, 2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}, 3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} are the position vectors of points A, B, C and D respectively, then find the angle between {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex}. Deduce that {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{C D}{/tex} are collinear.
solution_text: {tex}A(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mathrm{B}(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}), \mathrm{C}(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}D(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=(2-1) \hat{\imath}+(5-1) \hat{\jmath}+(0-1) \hat{k} \\ &=1 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{C D} &=(1-3) \hat{\imath}+(-6-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-1-(-3)) \hat{k} \\ &=-2 \hat{\imath}-8 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex} So, {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1+16+1} \sqrt{4+64+4}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-2-32-2}{\sqrt{18} \sqrt{72}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-36}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leq \theta \leq \pi{/tex} it follows that {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex} . This shows that {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} are collinear.
solution_html:
{tex}A(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}), \mathrm{B}(2 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}), \mathrm{C}(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}D(\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
 {tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{A B} &=(2-1) \hat{\imath}+(5-1) \hat{\jmath}+(0-1) \hat{k} \\ &=1 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{C D} &=(1-3) \hat{\imath}+(-6-2) \hat{\jmath}+(-1-(-3)) \hat{k} \\ &=-2 \hat{\imath}-8 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}{/tex}
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{CD}}{/tex}
So, {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{A B} \cdot \overrightarrow{C D}}{|\overrightarrow{A B}||\overrightarrow{C D}|}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{(\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(-2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{1+16+1} \sqrt{4+64+4}}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-2-32-2}{\sqrt{18} \sqrt{72}}{/tex}
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{-36}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 6 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = -1
cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos {tex}\pi{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} 
Since {tex}0 \leq \theta \leq \pi{/tex} it follows that {tex}\theta=\pi{/tex}. This shows that {tex}\vec {AB}{/tex} and {tex}\vec {CD}{/tex} are collinear.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 3,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 4,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 5{/tex} and each one of them being perpendicular to the sum of the other two, find {tex}\left| {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right|{/tex} .
question_html: Let {tex}\vec a,\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} be three vectors such that {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 3,\left| {\vec b} \right| = 4,\left| {\vec c} \right| = 5{/tex} and each one of them being perpendicular to the sum of the other two, find {tex}\left| {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right|{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}\vec a.\left( {\vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0,\vec b.\left( {\vec c + \vec a} \right) = 0,\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 0{/tex} (given) {tex}{\left| {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right|^2} = \left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right).\left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right){/tex} {tex}= \vec a.\vec a + \vec a\left( {\vec b + \vec c} \right) + \vec b.\vec b + \vec b\left( {\vec a + \vec c} \right) + \vec c.\vec c + \left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} {tex}= {\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} + {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} + {\left| {\vec c} \right|^2}{/tex} {tex} = 9 + 16 + 25{/tex} = 50 {tex}\left| {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right| = \sqrt {50} {/tex} {tex} = 5\sqrt 2 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec a.\left( {\vec b + \vec c} \right) = 0,\vec b.\left( {\vec c + \vec a} \right) = 0,\vec c.\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 0{/tex} (given)
{tex}{\left| {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right|^2} = \left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right).\left( {\vec a + \vec b + \vec c} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \vec a.\vec a + \vec a\left( {\vec b + \vec c} \right) + \vec b.\vec b + \vec b\left( {\vec a + \vec c} \right) + \vec c.\vec c + \left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Three vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} satisfy the condition {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0}{/tex} . Evaluate the quantity {tex}\mu=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex} , if {tex}|\vec{a}|=3,|\vec{b}|=4{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{c}|=2{/tex} .
question_html: Three vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} satisfy the condition {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0}{/tex}. Evaluate the quantity {tex}\mu=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}{/tex}, if {tex}|\vec{a}|=3,|\vec{b}|=4{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{c}|=2{/tex}.
solution_text: Since {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0}{/tex} , we have {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}=0{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=0{/tex} Therefore {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=-|\vec{a}|^{2}=-9{/tex} .....(i) Again {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})=0{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=-|\vec{b}|^{2}=-16{/tex} .....(ii) Similarly {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=-4{/tex} ......(iii) Adding (i), (ii) and (iii), we have {tex}2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})=-29{/tex} or {tex}2 \mu=-29{/tex} i.e. {tex}\mu=\frac{-29}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\vec{0}{/tex}, we have
{tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=\overrightarrow{0}=0{/tex} 
or {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=0{/tex} 
Therefore {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=-|\vec{a}|^{2}=-9{/tex} .....(i)
Again {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})=0{/tex} 
or {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=-|\vec{b}|^{2}=-16{/tex} .....(ii)
Similarly {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}=-4{/tex} ......(iii)
Adding (i), (ii) and (iii), we have
{tex}2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c})=-29{/tex} 
or {tex}2 \mu=-29{/tex} i.e. {tex}\mu=\frac{-29}{2}{/tex}

question_text: If with reference to the right handed system of mutually perpendicular unit vectors {tex}\hat i,\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\vec \alpha = 3\hat i - \hat j{/tex} , {tex}\vec \beta = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} , then express {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} in the form {tex}\vec \beta = {\vec \beta _1} + {\vec \beta _2}{/tex} , where {tex}{\vec \beta _1}{/tex} is || to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} and {tex}{\vec \beta _2}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} .
question_html: If with reference to the right handed system of mutually perpendicular unit vectors {tex}\hat i,\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\hat k{/tex}, {tex}\vec \alpha = 3\hat i - \hat j{/tex}, {tex}\vec \beta = 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}, then express {tex}\vec \beta {/tex} in the form {tex}\vec \beta = {\vec \beta _1} + {\vec \beta _2}{/tex}, where {tex}{\vec \beta _1}{/tex} is || to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex} and {tex}{\vec \beta _2}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec \alpha {/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda \vec \alpha \,\,\left[ {\because \,{{\vec \beta }_1}||to\,\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda \left( {3\hat i - \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex} {tex}= \left( {2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k} \right) - \left( {3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \left( {2 - 3\lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {1 + \lambda } \right)\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0\,\,\,\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex} {tex}3\left( {2 - 3\lambda } \right) - \left( {1 + \lambda } \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex} {tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda \vec \alpha \,\,\left[ {\because \,{{\vec \beta }_1}||to\,\,\vec \alpha } \right]{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \lambda \left( {3\hat i - \hat j} \right){/tex}
{tex} = 3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \vec \beta - {\vec \beta _1}{/tex}
{tex}= \left( {2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k} \right) - \left( {3\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \left( {2 - 3\lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {1 + \lambda } \right)\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec \alpha .{\vec \beta _2} = 0\,\,\,\left[ {\because {{\vec \beta }_2} \bot \vec \alpha } \right]{/tex}
{tex}3\left( {2 - 3\lambda } \right) - \left( {1 + \lambda } \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _1} = \frac{3}{2}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j{/tex}
{tex}{\vec \beta _2} = \frac{1}{2}\hat i + \frac{3}{2}\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Compute the magnitude of the following vectors: {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = 2\hat i - 7\hat j - 3\hat k,{/tex} {tex}\vec c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex}
question_html: Compute the magnitude of the following vectors: {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = 2\hat i - 7\hat j - 3\hat k,{/tex} {tex}\vec c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| {\vec a} \right| = \sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt 3 {/tex} And {tex}\vec b = 2\hat i - 7\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| {\vec b} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 7} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {4 + 49 + 9} = \sqrt {62} {/tex} Also {tex}\vec c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3}} = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \left| {\vec a} \right| = \sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {1^2}} = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt 3 {/tex}

And {tex}\vec b = 2\hat i - 7\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \left| {\vec b} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 7} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {4 + 49 + 9} = \sqrt {62} {/tex}

Also {tex}\vec c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat j - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \left| {\vec c} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3}} = 1{/tex}

{tex} = 0\hat i - 4\hat j - \hat k = - 4\hat j - \hat k {/tex}

question_text: Find the unit vector in the direction of the vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

solution_text: We know that a unit vector in the direction of the vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} is {tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {1 + 1 + 4} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat a = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that a unit vector in the direction of the vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}is

{tex}\hat a = \frac{{\vec a}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|}} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {1^2} + {2^2}} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {1 + 1 + 4} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt 6 }}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \hat a = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat j + \frac{2}{{\sqrt 6 }}\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find the unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} , where P and Q are the points (1, 2, 3) and (4, 5, 6), respectively
question_html: Find the unit vector in the direction of vector {tex}\overrightarrow {PQ} {/tex} , where P and Q are the points (1, 2, 3) and (4, 5, 6), respectively

options.explanation: {tex}\overrightarrow{PQ}=3\widehat{i}+3\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k}{/tex} , then {tex}\hat{PQ}=\frac{\vec{PQ}}{|\vec {PQ}|}=\frac{3\hat i +3 \hat j +3 \hat k}{\sqrt{3^2+3^2+3^2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3\hat i +3 \hat j +3 \hat k}{\sqrt{27}}=\frac{3(\hat i +\hat j +\hat j)}{3\sqrt3}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt3}\hat i+\frac{1}{\sqrt3}\hat j +\frac{1}{\sqrt 3}\hat k{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For given vectors, {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat j - \hat k{/tex} , find the unit vector in the direction of the vector {tex}\vec a + \vec b{/tex} .
question_html: For given vectors, {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat j - \hat k{/tex}, find the unit vector in the direction of the vector {tex}\vec a + \vec b{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a + \vec b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k - \hat i + \hat j - \hat k {/tex} {tex}= \hat i + 0\hat j + \hat k{/tex} Therefore, unit vector in the direction of {tex} {(\vec a + \vec b)} = \frac{{\hat i + 0\hat j + \hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( 1 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 0 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 1 \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\hat i + 0\hat j + \hat k}}{{\sqrt {1 + 0 + 1} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat k}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = - \hat i + \hat j - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec a + \vec b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k - \hat i + \hat j - \hat k {/tex} {tex}= \hat i + 0\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
Therefore, unit vector in the direction of {tex} {(\vec a + \vec b)} = \frac{{\hat i + 0\hat j + \hat k}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( 1 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 0 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 1 \right)}^2}} }}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{\hat i + 0\hat j + \hat k}}{{\sqrt {1 + 0 + 1} }} = \frac{{\hat i + \hat k}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat i + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}5\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} which has a magnitude of 8 units.
question_html: Find a vector in the direction of vector {tex}5\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} which has a magnitude of 8 units.

options.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a}=5\widehat{i}-\widehat{j}+2\widehat{k}{/tex} then, {tex}8\hat a = 8\frac{\vec a}{|\vec a|}{/tex} = {tex}8.\frac{5 \hat i-\hat j +2 \hat k}{\sqrt{5^2+(-1)^2+2^2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8(5 \hat i-\hat j +2 \hat k)}{\sqrt{30}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{40}{\sqrt{30}}\hat i -\frac{8}{\sqrt{30}}\hat j +\frac{16}{\sqrt{30}}\hat k{/tex}

question_text: Show that the vectors 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} and -4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 8 {tex}\hat k{/tex} are collinear.
question_html: Show that the vectors 2{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat k{/tex} and -4{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex} - 8{tex}\hat k{/tex} are collinear.
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = -4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 8 {tex}\hat k{/tex} . Now, {tex}\vec b{/tex} = -4 {tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - 8 {tex}\hat k{/tex} = -2(2 {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4 {tex}\hat k{/tex} ) = -2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} Thus, {tex}\vec b{/tex} = -2 {tex}\vec a{/tex} Hence, {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are collinear.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec a{/tex} = 2{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = -4{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex} - 8{tex}\hat k{/tex}.
Now, {tex}\vec b{/tex} = -4{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 6{tex}\hat j{/tex} - 8{tex}\hat k{/tex} = -2(2{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} + 4{tex}\hat k{/tex}) = -2{tex}\vec a{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\vec b{/tex} = -2{tex}\vec a{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are collinear.

question_text: Find the direction cosines of the vector {tex}\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
question_html: Find the direction cosines of the vector {tex}\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

options.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow a =\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k {/tex} , Then , {tex}\widehat a = \frac{{\overrightarrow a }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|}} = \frac{{\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {{1^2} + {2^2} + {3^2}} }} = \frac{{\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k}}{{\sqrt {14} }}{/tex} Therefore , the D.C.’s of vector a are : {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {14} }},\;\frac{2}{{\sqrt {14} }},\;\frac{3}{{\sqrt {14} }}.{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the direction cosines of the vector joining the points A(1, 2, -3) and B(-1, -2, 1) directed from A to B.
question_html: Find the direction cosines of the vector joining the points A(1, 2, -3) and B(-1, -2, 1) directed from A to B.
solution_text: Given: Points A(1, 2, -3) and B(-1, - 2,1) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of point {tex}A = \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} And Position vector of point {tex}B = \overrightarrow {OB} = - \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= - \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k - \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k {/tex} {tex}= - 2\hat i - 4\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} Now {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( 4 \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {4 + 16 + 16} = \sqrt {36} = 6{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A unit vector along {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|}} = \frac{{ - 2\hat i - 4\hat j + 4\hat k}}{6}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - 2}}{6}\hat i - \frac{{ 4}}{6}\hat j + \frac{4}{6}\hat k {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\hat i - \frac{{ 2}}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex} Therefore, the direction cosines of vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \frac{{ - 1}}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3},\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Points A(1, 2, -3) and B(-1, - 2,1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of point {tex}A = \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
And Position vector of point {tex}B = \overrightarrow {OB} = - \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= - \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k - \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k {/tex} {tex}= - 2\hat i - 4\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
Now {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( 4 \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {4 + 16 + 16} = \sqrt {36} = 6{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A unit vector along {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {AB} }}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|}} = \frac{{ - 2\hat i - 4\hat j + 4\hat k}}{6}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{ - 2}}{6}\hat i - \frac{{ 4}}{6}\hat j + \frac{4}{6}\hat k {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{3}\hat i - \frac{{ 2}}{3}\hat j + \frac{2}{3}\hat k{/tex}
Therefore, the direction cosines of vector {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \frac{{ - 1}}{3},\frac{{ - 2}}{3},\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

question_text: Show that the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} is equally inclined to the axes OX, OY and OZ.
question_html: Show that the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} is equally inclined to the axes OX, OY and OZ.
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} , then {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 1 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 1 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 1 \right)}^2}} = \sqrt 3 {/tex} Let us find angle {tex}{\theta _1}{/tex} (say) between vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} and OX {tex}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos {\theta _1} = \frac{{\vec a.\hat i}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|.\left| {\hat i} \right|}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {i + j + k} \right).\left( {\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k} \right)}}{{\left| {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right|\left| {\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k} \right|}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{1\left( 1 \right) + 1\left( 0 \right) + 1\left( 0 \right)}}{{\sqrt {1 + 1 + 1} .\sqrt {1 + 0 + 0} }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\theta _1} = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Similarly angle {tex}{\theta _2}{/tex} (say) between vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} and OY {tex}{/tex} is {tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} And angle {tex}{\theta _3}{/tex} (say) between vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} and OZ {tex}{/tex} is {tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\theta _1} = {\theta _2} = {\theta _3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} is equally inclined to OX, OY and OZ.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex}, then {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 1 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 1 \right)}^2} + {{\left( 1 \right)}^2}} = \sqrt 3 {/tex}

Let us find angle {tex}{\theta _1}{/tex} (say) between vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} and OX {tex}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \cos {\theta _1} = \frac{{\vec a.\hat i}}{{\left| {\vec a} \right|.\left| {\hat i} \right|}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {i + j + k} \right).\left( {\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k} \right)}}{{\left| {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right|\left| {\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k} \right|}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{1\left( 1 \right) + 1\left( 0 \right) + 1\left( 0 \right)}}{{\sqrt {1 + 1 + 1} .\sqrt {1 + 0 + 0} }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\theta _1} = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

Similarly angle {tex}{\theta _2}{/tex} (say) between vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} and OY {tex}{/tex} is {tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

And angle {tex}{\theta _3}{/tex} (say) between vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} and OZ {tex}{/tex} is {tex}{\cos ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore {\theta _1} = {\theta _2} = {\theta _3}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Vector {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} is equally inclined to OX, OY and OZ.

question_text: Find the position vector (internally) of a point R which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively, in the ratio 2 : 1.
question_html: Find the position vector (internally) of a point R which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively, in the ratio 2 : 1.
solution_text: Given that {tex}\vec{{P}}=\hat{1}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{Q}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} The {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} lies on the segment PQ(internal division). If {tex}\mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}{/tex} is the ratio in which {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} divides {tex}\mathrm{PQ}{/tex} then {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \vec{\mathrm{Q}}+\mathrm{n} \vec{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}}{/tex} Given m : n = 2 : 1, m = 2 and n = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2(-\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+1(\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{2+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \hat{1}+4 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}}{3}=\frac{-1}{3} \hat{i}+\frac{4}{3} \hat{\jmath}+\frac{1}{3} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\vec{{P}}=\hat{1}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{Q}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
The {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} lies on the segment PQ(internal division). If {tex}\mathrm{m}: \mathrm{n}{/tex} is the ratio in which {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} divides {tex}\mathrm{PQ}{/tex} then
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \vec{\mathrm{Q}}+\mathrm{n} \vec{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}}{/tex} 
Given m : n = 2 : 1, m = 2 and n = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2(-\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+1(\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{2+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1 \hat{1}+4 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}}{3}=\frac{-1}{3} \hat{i}+\frac{4}{3} \hat{\jmath}+\frac{1}{3} \hat{k}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the position vector (externally) of a point R which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively, in the ratio 2 : 1.
question_html: Find the position vector (externally) of a point R which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively, in the ratio 2 : 1.
solution_text: Given that {tex}\vec{{P}}=\hat{1}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{Q}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} The {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} does not lie on the segment PQ (external division). If {tex}\mathrm{m}: n{/tex} is the ratio in which {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} divides PQ, then {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{m \vec{\mathrm{Q}}-n \vec{\mathrm{P}}}{m-n}{/tex} Given m : n = 2 : 1, m = 2 and n = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})-1(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{2-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3 \hat{1}+0 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}}{1}=-3 \hat{1}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\vec{{P}}=\hat{1}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{Q}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
The {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} does not lie on the segment PQ (external division). If {tex}\mathrm{m}: n{/tex} is the ratio in which {tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} divides PQ, then
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{m \vec{\mathrm{Q}}-n \vec{\mathrm{P}}}{m-n}{/tex} 
Given m : n = 2 : 1, m = 2 and n = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})-1(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})}{2-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3 \hat{1}+0 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}}{1}=-3 \hat{1}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the position vector of the mid-point of the vector joining the points P (2, 3, 4) and Q(4, 1, -2).
question_html: Find the position vector of the mid-point of the vector joining the points P (2, 3, 4) and Q(4, 1, -2).
solution_text: Given: Point P (2, 3, 4) and Q(4,1, - 2) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of point P is {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} And Position vector of point Q is {tex}\vec b = 4\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} And Position vector of mid-point R of PQ is {tex}\frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{2} = \frac{{2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k + 4\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k}}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{6\hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k}}{2} = 3\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Point P (2, 3, 4) and Q(4,1, - 2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of point P is {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}
And Position vector of point Q is {tex}\vec b = 4\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
And Position vector of mid-point R of PQ is {tex}\frac{{\vec a + \vec b}}{2} = \frac{{2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k + 4\hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{6\hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k}}{2} = 3\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the points A, B and C with position vectors, {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively form the vertices of a right angled triangle.
question_html: Show that the points A, B and C with position vectors, {tex}\vec{a}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively form the vertices of a right angled triangle.
solution_text: Let O be the origin {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{OA}}=3 \hat{1}-4 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\vec{{OB}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{OC}}=\hat{1}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Now the find the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}, \vec{\mathrm{BC}}, \vec{\mathrm{CA}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}=\vec{\mathrm{B}}-\vec{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{AB}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})-(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{AB}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} ......(i) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{BC}}=\vec{\mathrm{C}}-\vec{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{{BC}}=(\hat{1}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{BC}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} ......(ii) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{CA}}=\vec{\mathrm{A}}-\vec{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{CA}}=(3 \hat{1}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{CA}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} ......(iii) Adding equation (i), (ii) and (iii) {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}+\vec{\mathrm{BC}}+\vec{\mathrm{CA}}{/tex} = {tex}(-\hat{1}+3 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k})+(-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k})+(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CA}}=0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Therefore, ABC form a triangle Now, we want to prove that is a right triangle. {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{AB}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(3)^{2}+5^{2}}=\sqrt{35}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{BC}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(-6)^{2}}=\sqrt{41}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{CA}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{(2)^{2}+(-1)^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{AB}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{CA}}|^{2}=(\sqrt{35})^{2}+(\sqrt{6})^{2}=35+6=41{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{AB}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{CA}}|^{2}=41=(\sqrt{41})^{2}=|\vec{\mathrm{BC}}|^{2}{/tex} Therefore, the triangle satisfies the Pythagoras theorem. Hence, proved the given vectors form a right angled triangle.
solution_html:
Let O be the origin
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{OA}}=3 \hat{1}-4 \hat{\jmath}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\vec{{OB}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
and 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{OC}}=\hat{1}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
Now the find the vectors {tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}, \vec{\mathrm{BC}}, \vec{\mathrm{CA}}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}=\vec{\mathrm{B}}-\vec{\mathrm{A}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{AB}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})-(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{AB}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} ......(i)
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{BC}}=\vec{\mathrm{C}}-\vec{\mathrm{B}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{{BC}}=(\hat{1}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{BC}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} ......(ii)
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{CA}}=\vec{\mathrm{A}}-\vec{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{CA}}=(3 \hat{1}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{CA}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} ......(iii)
Adding equation (i), (ii) and (iii)
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{AB}}+\vec{\mathrm{BC}}+\vec{\mathrm{CA}}{/tex} = {tex}(-\hat{1}+3 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k})+(-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k})+(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CA}}=0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
Therefore, ABC form a triangle
Now, we want to prove that is a right triangle.
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{AB}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(3)^{2}+5^{2}}=\sqrt{35}{/tex} 
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{BC}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(-6)^{2}}=\sqrt{41}{/tex} 
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{CA}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=\sqrt{(2)^{2}+(-1)^{2}+1^{2}}=\sqrt{6}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{AB}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{CA}}|^{2}=(\sqrt{35})^{2}+(\sqrt{6})^{2}=35+6=41{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{AB}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{CA}}|^{2}=41=(\sqrt{41})^{2}=|\vec{\mathrm{BC}}|^{2}{/tex} 
Therefore, the triangle satisfies the Pythagoras theorem. Hence, proved the given vectors form a right angled triangle.

question_text: Show that each of the given three vectors is a unit vector: {tex} \frac{1}{7}\left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right), \frac{1}{7}\left( {6\hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k} \right), \ \frac{1}{7}\left( {3\hat i - 6\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex} Also, show that they are mutually perpendicular to each other.
question_html:
Show that each of the given three vectors is a unit vector:
{tex} \frac{1}{7}\left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k} \right), \frac{1}{7}\left( {6\hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k} \right), \ \frac{1}{7}\left( {3\hat i - 6\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex} 
Also, show that they are mutually perpendicular to each other.
solution_text: {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| =\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{36+4+9}=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{49}= 1{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{36+4+9}=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{49} = 1{/tex} {tex}\left| {\vec c} \right|=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{9+36+4}=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{49} = 1{/tex} Hence they are unit vectors {tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{1}{49}(2\hat i+3\hat j+6\hat k)(6\hat i+2\hat j-3\hat k){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{49}(12+6-18)=0{/tex} {tex}\vec b.\vec c=\frac{1}{49}(6\hat i+2\hat j-3\hat k)(3\hat i-6\hat j+2\hat k){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{49}(18-12-6)=0{/tex} {tex}\vec c.\vec a = \frac{1}{49}(3\vec i-6\vec j+2\vec k)(2\vec i+3\vec j+6\vec k){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{49}(6-18+12)=0{/tex} {tex}\vec a \bot \vec b, \ \vec b \bot \vec c{/tex} and {tex}\vec c \bot \vec a{/tex} So they are {tex} \bot {/tex} to each other.
solution_html:
{tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| =\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{36+4+9}=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{49}= 1{/tex}

{tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{36+4+9}=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{49} = 1{/tex}

{tex}\left| {\vec c} \right|=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{9+36+4}=\frac{1}{7}\sqrt{49} = 1{/tex}

Hence they are unit vectors

{tex}\vec a.\vec b = \frac{1}{49}(2\hat i+3\hat j+6\hat k)(6\hat i+2\hat j-3\hat k){/tex}

{tex}=\frac{1}{49}(12+6-18)=0{/tex}

{tex}\vec b.\vec c=\frac{1}{49}(6\hat i+2\hat j-3\hat k)(3\hat i-6\hat j+2\hat k){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{1}{49}(18-12-6)=0{/tex}

{tex}\vec c.\vec a = \frac{1}{49}(3\vec i-6\vec j+2\vec k)(2\vec i+3\vec j+6\vec k){/tex}

{tex}=\frac{1}{49}(6-18+12)=0{/tex}

{tex}\vec a \bot \vec b, \ \vec b \bot \vec c{/tex} and {tex}\vec c \bot \vec a{/tex}

So they are {tex} \bot {/tex} to each other.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right|{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex} if {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) = 8{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 8\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right|{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex} if {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) = 8{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 8\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) = 8{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 8\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a.\vec a - \vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec a - \vec b.\vec b = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} - \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec b - {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} - \left| {\vec b} \right|^2 = 8{/tex} ...(ii) Putting {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} = 64\left| {\vec b} \right||^2{/tex} in eq. (ii), {tex}64{\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} - \left| {\vec b} \right|^2 = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {64 - 1} \right){\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {63} \right){\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = \frac{8}{{63}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = \sqrt {\frac{8}{{63}}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{{3\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} Putting {tex}{\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{{3\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} in eq (i), {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} = 8\left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{{3\sqrt 7 }}} \right) = \frac{{16}}{3}\sqrt {\frac{2}{7}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right).\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) = 8{/tex} and {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = 8\left| {\vec b} \right|{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}\Rightarrow \vec a.\vec a - \vec a.\vec b + \vec b.\vec a - \vec b.\vec b = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} - \vec a.\vec b + \vec a.\vec b - {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} - \left| {\vec b} \right|^2 = 8{/tex} ...(ii)

Putting {tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} = 64\left| {\vec b} \right||^2{/tex} in eq. (ii),

{tex}64{\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} - \left| {\vec b} \right|^2 = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {64 - 1} \right){\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = 8{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {63} \right){\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = \frac{8}{{63}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = \sqrt {\frac{8}{{63}}} = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{{3\sqrt 7 }}{/tex}

Putting {tex}{\left| {\vec b} \right|^2} = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{{3\sqrt 7 }}{/tex} in eq (i),

{tex}{\left| {\vec a} \right|^2} = 8\left( {\frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{{3\sqrt 7 }}} \right) = \frac{{16}}{3}\sqrt {\frac{2}{7}} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the magnitude of two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} , having the same magnitude and such that the angle between them is 60 o and their scalar product is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the magnitude of two vectors {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}, having the same magnitude and such that the angle between them is 60o and their scalar product is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} Given, magnitude of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} = magnitude of vector {tex}\vec b{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} and {tex}\theta=60^{\circ}{/tex} Scalar product of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} We know, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2} \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2} \cos 60^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=1{/tex} [magnitude of vector is positive] So, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=1{/tex} Hence, {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=1{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}
Given, magnitude of vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} = magnitude of vector {tex}\vec b{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|{/tex} and {tex}\theta=60^{\circ}{/tex}
Scalar product of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
We know, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2} \cos \theta{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2} \cos 60^{\circ}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}=|\vec{a}|^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=1{/tex} [magnitude of vector is positive]
So, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=1{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=1{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}|\vec{x}|{/tex} , if for a unit vector {tex}\vec{a},(\vec{x}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{x}+\vec{a})=12{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}|\vec{x}|{/tex}, if for a unit vector {tex}\vec{a},(\vec{x}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{x}+\vec{a})=12{/tex}
solution_text: Given, vector {tex}\vec a {/tex} is a unit vector {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=1{/tex} {tex}(\vec{x}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{x}+\vec{a})=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{x}}+\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{x}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}+\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}-|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=12{/tex} [Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}-|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}-1^{2}=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}=12+1=13{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|=\sqrt{13}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|=\sqrt{13}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, vector {tex}\vec a {/tex} is a unit vector
{tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=1{/tex} 
{tex}(\vec{x}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{x}+\vec{a})=12{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{x}}+\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{x}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=12{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}+\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}-\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}-|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=12{/tex} [Since {tex}\vec{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{x}}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}-|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=12{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}-1^{2}=12{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|^{2}=12+1=13{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|=\sqrt{13}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{x}}|=\sqrt{13}{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} are such that {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} , then find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex}
question_html: If {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} are such that {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex}, then find the value of {tex}\lambda {/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} Now {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k + \lambda \left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} = {tex} = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k - \lambda \hat i + 2\lambda \hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec a + \lambda \vec b = \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {2 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex} Again, {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} Since, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right){/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} therefore, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right).\vec c = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 - 3\lambda + 2 + 2\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow - \lambda + 8 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - \lambda = - 8 \Rightarrow \lambda = 8{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k,\vec b = - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j{/tex} 

Now {tex}\vec a + \lambda \vec b = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k + \lambda \left( { - \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}=

{tex} = 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k - \lambda \hat i + 2\lambda \hat j + \lambda \hat k{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \vec a + \lambda \vec b = \left( {2 - \lambda } \right)\hat i + \left( {2 + 2\lambda } \right)\hat j + \left( {3 + \lambda } \right)\hat k{/tex}

Again, {tex}\vec c = 3\hat i + \hat j = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex}

Since, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right){/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\vec c{/tex} therefore, {tex}\left( {\vec a + \lambda \vec b} \right).\vec c = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 6 - 3\lambda + 2 + 2\lambda = 0 \Rightarrow - \lambda + 8 = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow - \lambda = - 8 \Rightarrow \lambda = 8{/tex}

solution_text: To show {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} for {tex}\vec a \neq0{/tex} and {tex}\vec b \neq 0{/tex} , we need to show Dot product of {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} is zero. {tex}(|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}) \cdot(|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}){/tex} = {tex}(|\vec{a}| \vec{b}) \cdot(|\vec{a}| \vec{b})-(|\vec{a}| \vec{b}) \cdot(|\vec{b}| \vec{a})+(|\vec{b}| \vec{a}) \cdot(|\vec{a}| \vec{b})-(|\vec{b}| \vec{a}) \cdot(|\vec{b}| \vec{a}){/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2} \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2} \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}{/tex} [Since, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} ] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex}
solution_html:
To show {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} for  {tex}\vec a \neq0{/tex} and {tex}\vec b \neq 0{/tex},
we need to show Dot product of  {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex}  is zero.
{tex}(|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}) \cdot(|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}){/tex} 
= {tex}(|\vec{a}| \vec{b}) \cdot(|\vec{a}| \vec{b})-(|\vec{a}| \vec{b}) \cdot(|\vec{b}| \vec{a})+(|\vec{b}| \vec{a}) \cdot(|\vec{a}| \vec{b})-(|\vec{b}| \vec{a}) \cdot(|\vec{b}| \vec{a}){/tex} 
= {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2} \vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2} \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} 
= {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}{/tex} [Since, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex}]
= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \vec{\mathrm{b}}-|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex}
