question_text: If either vector {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex} , then {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 0. But the converse need not be true. Justify your answer with an example.
question_html: If either vector {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex}, then {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 0. But the converse need not be true. Justify your answer with an example.
solution_text: If either {tex}\vec{a}=0{/tex} or {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} then {tex}\vec{a} . \vec{b}=0{/tex} Now let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{1}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{1}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{1}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{1} .3 \hat{1}-2 \hat{1} \cdot 6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{1} \cdot 2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \cdot 3 \hat{1}{/tex} - {tex}3 \hat{0} \cdot 6 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\jmath} .2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} .3 \hat{1}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} .6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} .2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 6 - 0 + 0 + 0 - 18 + 0 + 0 - 0 + 12 [ {tex}\hat{ {1}} \hat{ {j}}=\hat{ {j}} \cdot \hat{ {k}}=\hat{ {k}} \cdot \hat{ {\imath}}=0{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 18 - 18 = 0 [ {tex}\text { i. }\hat{\mathrm{i}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\hat{\mathrm{k}}. \hat{\mathrm{k}}=1{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0~ for ~\vec{\mathrm{a}} \neq 0, \vec{\mathrm{b}} \neq 0{/tex} Hence, the converse of given statement need not be true.
solution_html:
If either {tex}\vec{a}=0{/tex} or {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} then {tex}\vec{a} . \vec{b}=0{/tex} 
Now let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{1}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{1}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{1}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{1} .3 \hat{1}-2 \hat{1} \cdot 6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{1} \cdot 2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \cdot 3 \hat{1}{/tex} - {tex}3 \hat{0} \cdot 6 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{\jmath} .2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} .3 \hat{1}-6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} .6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} .2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 6 - 0 + 0 + 0 - 18 + 0 + 0 - 0 + 12 [{tex}\hat{ {1}} \hat{ {j}}=\hat{ {j}} \cdot \hat{ {k}}=\hat{ {k}} \cdot \hat{ {\imath}}=0{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex} = 18 - 18 = 0  [{tex}\text { i. }\hat{\mathrm{i}}=\hat{\mathrm{j}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\hat{\mathrm{k}}. \hat{\mathrm{k}}=1{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0~ for ~\vec{\mathrm{a}} \neq 0, \vec{\mathrm{b}} \neq 0{/tex}
Hence, the converse of given statement need not be true.

question_text: Show that the points A (1, 2, 7), B (2, 6, 3) and C(3, 10, -1) are collinear.
question_html: Show that the points A (1, 2, 7), B (2, 6, 3) and C(3, 10, -1) are collinear.
solution_text: The given points are A (1, 2, 7), B (2, 6, 3) and C(3,10, - 1) respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of point {tex}A = \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} Position vector of point {tex}B = \overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} Position vector of point {tex}C = \overrightarrow {OC} = 3\hat i + 10\hat j - \hat k{/tex} Now {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} = Position vector of point B – Position vector of point A {tex}= 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k - \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 7\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k - \hat i - 2\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \hat i + 4\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} …(i) And {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} = Position vector of point C – Position vector of point A {tex}= 3\hat i + 10\hat j - \hat k - \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 7\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}= 3\hat i + 10\hat j - \hat k - \hat i - 2\hat j -7\hat k {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 8\hat j - 8\hat k = 2\left( {\hat i + 4\hat j - 4\hat k} \right){/tex} …(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {AC} = 2.\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} [Using eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Vectors {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} are parallel. {tex}\left[ {\because \vec a = mb} \right]{/tex} But {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} have a common point A and hence they can’t be parallel. Thus, the points A, B, C are collinear.
solution_html:
The given points are A (1, 2, 7), B (2, 6, 3) and C(3,10, - 1) respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Position vector of point {tex}A = \overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}
Position vector of point {tex}B = \overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
Position vector of point {tex}C = \overrightarrow {OC} = 3\hat i + 10\hat j - \hat k{/tex}
Now {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} = Position vector of point B – Position vector of point A
{tex}= 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k - \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 7\hat k} \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2\hat i + 6\hat j + 3\hat k - \hat i - 2\hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex} = \hat i + 4\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex}…(i)
And {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} = Position vector of point C – Position vector of point A
{tex}= 3\hat i + 10\hat j - \hat k - \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 7\hat k} \right){/tex}
{tex}= 3\hat i + 10\hat j - \hat k - \hat i - 2\hat j -7\hat k {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 8\hat j - 8\hat k = 2\left( {\hat i + 4\hat j - 4\hat k} \right){/tex}…(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {AC} = 2.\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} [Using eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Vectors {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} are parallel. {tex}\left[ {\because \vec a = mb} \right]{/tex}
But {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow {AC} {/tex} have a common point A and hence they can’t be parallel. Thus, the points A, B, C are collinear.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} from the vertices of a right angled triangle.
question_html: Show that the vectors {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} from the vertices of a right angled triangle.
solution_text: {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = - \hat i - 2\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {CA} = - \hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} = 1^2+2^2+6^2=41{/tex} {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2 = 2^2+(-1)^2+1^2=6{/tex} {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2 = (-1)^2+3^2+5^2=35{/tex} Here, {tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} = {\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2} + {\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2}{/tex} Hence, the {tex}\Delta {/tex} is a right angled triangle.
solution_html:
{tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = - \hat i - 2\hat j - 6\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow {CA} = - \hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} = 1^2+2^2+6^2=41{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2 = 2^2+(-1)^2+1^2=6{/tex}
{tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2 = (-1)^2+3^2+5^2=35{/tex}
Here, {tex}{\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} } \right|^2} = {\left| {\overrightarrow {BC} } \right|^2} + {\left| {\overrightarrow {CA} } \right|^2}{/tex}
Hence, the {tex}\Delta {/tex} is a right angled triangle.

question_text: Find {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex} , if {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} .
question_html: Find {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|{/tex}, if {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}.
solution_text: Given that {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{1}-7 \hat{\jmath}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{1}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{1}} & {\hat{\jmath}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {-7} & {7} \\ {3} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right|{/tex} Expanding along first row, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}[(-7 \times 2)-(-2 \times 7)]-\hat{\mathrm{j}}[(1 \times 2)-(7 \times 3)]+\hat{\mathrm{k}}[(-2 \times 1)-(-7 \times 3)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=(0) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(-19) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(19) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=0 \hat{i}+19 \hat{\jmath}+19 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{0^{2}+19^{2}+19^{2}}=19 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{1}-7 \hat{\jmath}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{1}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{1}} & {\hat{\jmath}} & {\hat{k}} \\ {1} & {-7} & {7} \\ {3} & {-2} & {2} \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
Expanding along first row,
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}[(-7 \times 2)-(-2 \times 7)]-\hat{\mathrm{j}}[(1 \times 2)-(7 \times 3)]+\hat{\mathrm{k}}[(-2 \times 1)-(-7 \times 3)]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=(0) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(-19) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(19) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=0 \hat{i}+19 \hat{\jmath}+19 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore|\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}|=\sqrt{0^{2}+19^{2}+19^{2}}=19 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

question_text: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vector {tex}\vec a + \vec b\;{/tex} and {tex}\vec a - \vec b{/tex} , where {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k\;{/tex} and {tex}\;\vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} .
question_html: Find a unit vector perpendicular to each of the vector {tex}\vec a + \vec b\;{/tex} and {tex}\vec a - \vec b{/tex}, where {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k\;{/tex} and {tex}\;\vec b = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}.

options.explanation: It is given that: {tex}\vec a = 3\hat i+2 \hat j+2 \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b=\hat i +2 \hat j-2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec a +\vec b=4\hat i+4\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\vec a -\vec b=2\hat i +4 \hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore (\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 4&4&0 \\ 2&0&4 \end{array}} \right| = 16\hat i - 16\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore| (\vec a + \vec b) \times (\vec a - \vec b) |= \sqrt{576}=24{/tex} Therefore, the unit vector perpendicular to both the vectors {tex}(\overrightarrow{a}+\overrightarrow{b}){/tex} and {tex}(\overrightarrow{a}-\overrightarrow{b}){/tex} is given by: {tex}=\pm \frac{(16\widehat{i}-16\widehat{j}-8\widehat{k})}{24}=\pm \frac{1}{3}(2\widehat{i}-2\widehat{j}-\widehat{k}) .{/tex}

question_text: If a unit vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} makes angles {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} with {tex}\hat i{/tex} , {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} with {tex}\hat j{/tex} and an acute angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with {tex}\hat k{/tex} , then find {tex}\theta{/tex} and hence, the components of {tex}\vec a {/tex} .
question_html: If a unit vector {tex}\vec a{/tex} makes angles {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} with {tex}\hat i{/tex}, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} with {tex}\hat j{/tex} and an acute angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with {tex}\hat k{/tex}, then find {tex}\theta{/tex} and hence, the components of {tex}\vec a {/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Given that it is a unit vector, So, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=1{/tex} ......(i) Let {tex}\alpha, \beta, \theta{/tex} be the angles {tex}\vec a{/tex} makes with {tex}\hat{i}, \hat{\jmath}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} respectively. Then, {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \hat{\imath}}{|\vec{a}||\hat{\imath}|}=\frac{x}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} (Given) {tex}\Rightarrow x=\cos \alpha=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\cos \beta=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\hat{\mathrm{j}}|}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \beta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} (Given) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos \beta=\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\hat{\mathrm{k}}|}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {z}=\cos \theta{/tex} Putting value of x,y, z in equation (i) {tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=1{/tex} (Squaring both sides) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+(\cos \theta)^{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \theta=1-\frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex} As {tex}\theta{/tex} should be acute, {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{z}=\cos \theta=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Components of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} are the coefficients of {tex}\hat{\imath}, \hat{\jmath}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} which are {tex}x=\frac{1}{2} ; y=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} ; z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} with {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
Given that it is a unit vector,
So, {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}}=1{/tex} ......(i)
Let {tex}\alpha, \beta, \theta{/tex} be the angles  {tex}\vec a{/tex} makes with {tex}\hat{i}, \hat{\jmath}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} respectively.
Then,
{tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot \hat{\imath}}{|\vec{a}||\hat{\imath}|}=\frac{x}{1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\cos \alpha=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\cos \beta=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\hat{\mathrm{j}}|}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \beta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} (Given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos \beta=\cos \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}}||\hat{\mathrm{k}}|}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {z}=\cos \theta{/tex} 
Putting value of x,y, z in equation (i)
{tex}\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=1{/tex} (Squaring both sides)
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+(\cos \theta)^{2}=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}+\cos ^{2} \theta=1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \theta=1-\frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\pm \frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
As {tex}\theta{/tex} should be acute, {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{z}=\cos \theta=\cos \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Components of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} are the coefficients of {tex}\hat{\imath}, \hat{\jmath}, \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} which are 
{tex}x=\frac{1}{2} ; y=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} ; z=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} with {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

question_text: Show that {tex}\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) \times \left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 2\left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex}
question_html: Show that {tex}\left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) \times \left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right) = 2\left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex}
solution_text: L.H.S {tex}= \left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) \times \left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} {tex}= \vec a \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b - \vec b \times \vec a - \vec b \times \vec b{/tex} {tex}= 0 + \vec a \times \vec b + \vec a \times \vec b - 0{/tex} {tex}[as \ \vec a×\vec b=-\vec b×\ \vec a]{/tex} {tex}=2(\vec a×\vec b){/tex}
solution_html:
L.H.S {tex}= \left( {\vec a - \vec b} \right) \times \left( {\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \vec a \times \vec a + \vec a \times \vec b - \vec b \times \vec a - \vec b \times \vec b{/tex}

{tex}= 0 + \vec a \times \vec b + \vec a \times \vec b - 0{/tex}    {tex}[as \ \vec a×\vec b=-\vec b×\ \vec a]{/tex}

{tex}=2(\vec a×\vec b){/tex}

question_text: Find {tex}\lambda {/tex} and {tex}\mu {/tex} if {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = \overrightarrow 0 {/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}\lambda {/tex} and {tex}\mu {/tex} if {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = \overrightarrow 0 {/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&6&{27} \\ 1&\lambda &u \end{array}} \right| = \vec 0{/tex} Expanding along first row, {tex}\hat i\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) - \hat j\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \vec 0 = 0\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i,\hat j,\hat k{/tex} on both sides, we have {tex}6\mu - 27\lambda = 0{/tex} …(i) {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0{/tex} …(ii) And {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0{/tex} ...(iii) From eq. (ii), {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} From eq. (iii), {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex} Putting the values of {tex}\mu {/tex} and {tex}\lambda {/tex} in eq. (i), {tex}6\left( {\frac{{27}}{2}} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {27} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 =0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mu = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda = 3.{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left( {2\hat i + 6\hat j + 27\hat k} \right) \times \left( {\hat i + \lambda \hat j + u\hat k} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&6&{27} \\ 1&\lambda &u \end{array}} \right| = \vec 0{/tex}

Expanding along first row,

{tex}\hat i\left( {6\mu - 27\lambda } \right) - \hat j\left( {2\mu - 27} \right) + \hat k\left( {2\lambda - 6} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \vec 0 = 0\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex}

Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i,\hat j,\hat k{/tex} on both sides, we have

{tex}6\mu - 27\lambda = 0{/tex} …(i)

{tex}2\mu - 27 = 0{/tex} …(ii)

And {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0{/tex} ...(iii)

From eq. (ii), {tex}2\mu - 27 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex}

From eq. (iii), {tex}2\lambda - 6 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{6}{2} = 3{/tex}

Putting the values of {tex}\mu {/tex} and {tex}\lambda {/tex} in eq. (i),

{tex}6\left( {\frac{{27}}{2}} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {27} \right) - 27\left( 3 \right) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 =0{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\mu = \frac{{27}}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda = 3.{/tex}

question_text: Given that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex} . What can you conclude about the vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} ?
question_html: Given that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\overrightarrow{0}{/tex}. What can you conclude about the vectors {tex}\vec a {/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}?
solution_text: Given that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \cos \theta=0{/tex} (where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the vectors) {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}\cos \theta=0{/tex} Also given that, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \sin \theta=0{/tex} (where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the vectors) {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}\sin \theta=0{/tex} As there is no value of {tex}\theta{/tex} for which both sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} are zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The condition for which {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} is; Either {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \cos \theta=0{/tex} (where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the vectors)
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}\cos \theta=0{/tex} 
Also given that, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{b}}| \cdot \sin \theta=0{/tex} (where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the vectors)
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}\sin \theta=0{/tex} 
As there is no value of {tex}\theta{/tex} for which both sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and cos {tex}\theta{/tex} are zero.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The condition for which {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=0{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} is;
Either {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=0{/tex} or {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=0{/tex}

question_text: Let the vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} be given as {tex}a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k},~ b_{1} \hat{i}+b_{2} \hat{j}+b_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} , and {tex}c_{1} \hat{i}+c_{2} \hat{j}+c_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} . Then show that {tex}\vec{a} \times(\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex}
question_html: Let the vectors {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}{/tex} be given as {tex}a_{1} \hat{i}+a_{2} \hat{j}+a_{3} \hat{k},~ b_{1} \hat{i}+b_{2} \hat{j}+b_{3} \hat{k}{/tex}, and {tex}c_{1} \hat{i}+c_{2} \hat{j}+c_{3} \hat{k}{/tex} . Then show that {tex}\vec{a} \times(\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex}
solution_text: Given that {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{b}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{b}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{b}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{c}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{c}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{c}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}We ~have~to~show,~\vec{a} \times(\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex} Solving for left hand side, {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\left(\mathrm{b}_{1}+\mathrm{c}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{b}_{2}+\mathrm{c}_{2}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(\mathrm{b}_{3}+\mathrm{c}_{3}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times(\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{\mathrm{i}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {\mathrm{a}_{1}} & {\mathrm{a}_{2}} & {\mathrm{a}_{3}} \\ {\mathrm{b}_{1}+\mathrm{c}_{1}} & {\mathrm{b}_{2}+\mathrm{c}_{2}} & {\mathrm{b}_{3}+\mathrm{c}_{3}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times(\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\hat{\mathrm{i}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {\mathrm{a}_{1}} & {\mathrm{a}_{2}} & {\mathrm{a}_{3}} \\ {\mathrm{b}_{1}} & {\mathrm{b}_{2}} & {\mathrm{b}_{3}} \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{\mathrm{i}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {\mathrm{a}_{1}} & {\mathrm{a}_{2}} & {\mathrm{a}_{3}} \\ {\mathrm{c}_{1}} & {\mathrm{c}_{2}} & {\mathrm{c}_{3}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} (property of determinant) {tex}=(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}})+(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}){/tex} = R.H.S. {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}})=(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}})+(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}){/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given that
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}=\mathrm{b}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{b}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{b}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{c}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{c}_{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{c}_{3} \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}We ~have~to~show,~\vec{a} \times(\vec{b}+\vec{c})=\vec{a} \times \vec{b}+\vec{a} \times \vec{c}{/tex}
Solving for left hand side,
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}=\left(\mathrm{b}_{1}+\mathrm{c}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{b}_{2}+\mathrm{c}_{2}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(\mathrm{b}_{3}+\mathrm{c}_{3}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times(\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{\mathrm{i}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {\mathrm{a}_{1}} & {\mathrm{a}_{2}} & {\mathrm{a}_{3}} \\ {\mathrm{b}_{1}+\mathrm{c}_{1}} & {\mathrm{b}_{2}+\mathrm{c}_{2}} & {\mathrm{b}_{3}+\mathrm{c}_{3}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \times(\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}})=\left|\begin{array}{lll} {\hat{\mathrm{i}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {\mathrm{a}_{1}} & {\mathrm{a}_{2}} & {\mathrm{a}_{3}} \\ {\mathrm{b}_{1}} & {\mathrm{b}_{2}} & {\mathrm{b}_{3}} \end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ccc} {\hat{\mathrm{i}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{j}}} & {\hat{\mathrm{k}}} \\ {\mathrm{a}_{1}} & {\mathrm{a}_{2}} & {\mathrm{a}_{3}} \\ {\mathrm{c}_{1}} & {\mathrm{c}_{2}} & {\mathrm{c}_{3}} \end{array}\right|{/tex} (property of determinant)
{tex}=(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}})+(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}){/tex} = R.H.S.
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}})=(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}})+(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \times \vec{\mathrm{c}}){/tex}
Hence proved.

question_text: If either {tex}\vec{a}=\vec {0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex} , then {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec 0{/tex} . Is the converse true? Justify your answer with an example.
question_html: If either {tex}\vec{a}=\vec {0}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex}, then {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec 0{/tex}. Is the converse true? Justify your answer with an example.
solution_text: Firstly let us consider, Take any parallel non-zero vectors so that {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex} Let {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} Then, {tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 & 8\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}(24-24)-\hat{j}(16-16)+\hat{k}(12-12){/tex} = {tex}0 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}=\vec{0}{/tex} Now, it's seen that {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{29}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a} \neq \vec{0}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4^{2}+6^{2}+8^{2}}=\sqrt{116}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{b} \neq \vec{0} {/tex} Thus, the converse of the given statement need not be true.
solution_html:
Firstly let us consider, Take any parallel non-zero vectors so that
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{0}{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex}
Then,
{tex}\vec{a} \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 6 & 8\end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat{i}(24-24)-\hat{j}(16-16)+\hat{k}(12-12){/tex} = {tex}0 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+0 \hat{k}=\vec{0}{/tex}
Now, it's seen that {tex}|\vec{a}|=\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}}=\sqrt{29}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{a} \neq \vec{0}{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4^{2}+6^{2}+8^{2}}=\sqrt{116}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{b} \neq \vec{0} {/tex}
Thus, the converse of the given statement need not be true.

question_text: Find the area of the triangle with vertices A(1, 1, 2), B(2, 3, 5) and C(1, 5, 5).
question_html: Find the area of the triangle with vertices A(1, 1, 2), B(2, 3, 5) and C(1, 5, 5).

options.explanation: Given position vector of A , {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = \hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} position vector of B, {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} and that of C, {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i + 5\hat j + 5\hat k{/tex} therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + 5\hat k} \right) - \left( {\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right) = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} (by triangle law of vector addition) thus we may write {tex}\vec{AB}=\hat i +2\hat j+3\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\vec{AC}=4\hat j+3\hat k,{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{AB}\times \overrightarrow{AC}=\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&3 \\ 0&4&3 \end{array}} \right|=-6\hat i-3\hat j+4\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\overrightarrow{AB}\times\vec{AC}|=\sqrt{61}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{1}{2} |\overrightarrow{AB}\times\vec{AC}|=\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{61}{/tex} Therefore, the area of triangle ABC is = {tex}{1 \over 2}\sqrt {61} {/tex}

question_html: Area of a rectangle having vertices A, B, C and D with position vectors {tex} - \hat i + \frac{1}{2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}, {tex}\hat i + \frac{1}{2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}, {tex}\hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}- \hat i - \frac{1}{2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}, respectively is

options.explanation: We have: {tex}​​\overrightarrow {OA} = - \hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} (position vector of A) similarly , {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = \hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = \hat i - {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\overrightarrow {OD} = - \hat i - {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} : ,where {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} = \left( {\hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) - \left( { - \hat i + {1 \over 2}\hat j + 4\hat k} \right) = 2\hat i + 0\hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} (by triangle law of vector addition),similarly {tex}\overrightarrow {AD} = 0\hat i - \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} , Therefore , area of rectangle ABCD is given by {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} X} \right.\left. {\overrightarrow {AD} } \right|{/tex} ,where {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} X\overrightarrow {AD} = {/tex} {tex}\left| {\matrix{ {\hat i} & {\hat j} & {\hat k} \cr 2 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & { - 1} & 0 \cr } } \right| = \hat i\left( {0 - 0} \right) - \hat j\left( {0 - 0} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 0} \right){/tex} = {tex} - 2\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {AB} X} \right.\left. {\overrightarrow {AD} } \right|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt {{0^2} + {0^2} + {{\left( { - 2} \right)}^2}} = {/tex} 2 sq. units.

question_text: Find the scalar components and magnitude of the vector joining the points P(x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) and Q (x 2 , y 2 , z 2 )
question_html: Find the scalar components and magnitude of the vector joining the points P(x1, y1, z1) and Q (x2, y2, z2)
solution_text: Given points are P(x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) and Q(x 2 , y 2 , z 2 ). The vector obtained by joining the given points P and Q is: {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} = position vector of Q - position vector of P = {tex}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-\mathrm{z}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-\mathrm{z}_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Hence, the scalar components and the magnitude of the vector joining the given points P & Q are {(x 2 - x 1 ), (y 2 - y 1 ), (z 2 - z 1 )} and {tex}\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex} respectively.
solution_html:
Given points are P(x1, y1, z1) and Q(x2, y2, z2).
The vector obtained by joining the given points P and Q is:
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} = position vector of Q - position vector of P
= {tex}\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-\mathrm{z}_{1}\right) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}|\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}|=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-\mathrm{z}_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
Hence, the scalar components and the magnitude of the vector joining the given points P & Q are {(x2 - x1), (y2 - y1), (z2 - z1)} and {tex}\sqrt{\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right)^{2}}{/tex} respectively.

question_text: If {tex}\vec a = \vec b + \vec c{/tex} , then is it true that {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex} ? Justify your answer.
question_html: If {tex}\vec a = \vec b + \vec c{/tex}, then is it true that {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex}? Justify your answer.
solution_text: Given: {tex}\vec a = \vec b + \vec c{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Either the vectors {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are collinear or form the sides of a triangle. Case I: Vectors {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are collinear. Let {tex}\vec a = \overrightarrow {AC} ,\vec b = \overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \overrightarrow {BC} {/tex} Then {tex}\vec a = \overrightarrow {AC} = \overrightarrow {AB} + \overrightarrow {BC} = \vec b + \vec c{/tex} Also, {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = AC = AB + BC = \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex} Case II: Vectors {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} form a triangle. Here also by Triangle Law of vectors, {tex}\vec a = \vec b + \vec c{/tex} But {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| < \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} Each side of a triangle is less than sum of the other two sides] {tex}\therefore \,\left| {\vec a = \vec b + \vec c} \right| = \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex} is true only when vectors {tex}\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} are collinear vectors.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\vec a = \vec b + \vec c{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Either the vectors {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are collinear or form the sides of a triangle.
Case I: Vectors {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} are collinear.
Let {tex}\vec a = \overrightarrow {AC} ,\vec b = \overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \overrightarrow {BC} {/tex}
Then {tex}\vec a = \overrightarrow {AC} = \overrightarrow {AB} + \overrightarrow {BC} = \vec b + \vec c{/tex}
Also, {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| = AC = AB + BC = \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex}
Case II: Vectors {tex}\vec a,\vec b,\vec c{/tex} form a triangle.
Here also by Triangle Law of vectors, {tex}\vec a = \vec b + \vec c{/tex}
But {tex}\left| {\vec a} \right| < \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} Each side of a triangle is less than sum of the other two sides]
{tex}\therefore \,\left| {\vec a = \vec b + \vec c} \right| = \left| {\vec b} \right| + \left| {\vec c} \right|{/tex} is true only when vectors {tex}\vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex}are collinear vectors.

question_text: Find a vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel to the resultant of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
question_html: Find a vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel to the resultant of the vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
solution_text: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} Let vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} be the resultant vector of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec c = \vec a + \vec b {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k + \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel (or collinear) to resultant vector {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex} is {tex}5\hat c = 5\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 1 + 0} }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }} \times \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{5\sqrt {10} }}{{10}}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5\hat c = \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\left( {3\vec i + \vec j} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{{3\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat i + \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat j{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}

Let vector {tex}\vec c{/tex} be the resultant vector of {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \vec c = \vec a + \vec b {/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 3\hat j - \hat k + \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}

{tex} = 3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required vector of magnitude 5 units and parallel (or collinear) to resultant vector {tex}\vec c = \vec a + \vec b{/tex} is

{tex}5\hat c = 5\frac{{\vec c}}{{\left| {\vec c} \right|}} = 5\left( {\frac{{3\hat i + \hat j + 0\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 1 + 0} }}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{5}{{\sqrt {10} }} \times \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{{\sqrt {10} }}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{5\sqrt {10} }}{{10}}\left( {3\hat i + \hat j} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 5\hat c = \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\left( {3\vec i + \vec j} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{3\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat i + \frac{{\sqrt {10} }}{2}\hat j{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} find a unit vector parallel to the vector {tex}2\vec a - \vec b + 3\vec c{/tex}
question_html: If {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} find a unit vector parallel to the vector {tex}2\vec a - \vec b + 3\vec c{/tex}
solution_text: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} Let {tex}\vec d = 2\vec a - \vec b + 3\vec c{/tex} {tex}= 2\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right) - \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + 3\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k - 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k + 3\hat i - 6\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A unit vector parallel to the vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} is {tex}\hat d = \frac{{\vec d}}{{\left| {\vec d} \right|}} = \frac{{3\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 9 + 4} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{3\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {22} }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{3}{{\sqrt {22} }}\hat i - \frac{3}{{\sqrt {22} }}\hat j + \frac{2}{{\sqrt {22} }}\hat k{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,\vec b = 2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
Let {tex}\vec d = 2\vec a - \vec b + 3\vec c{/tex}
{tex}= 2\left( {\hat i + \hat j + \hat k} \right) - \left( {2\hat i - \hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + 3\left( {\hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2\hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k - 2\hat i + \hat j - 3\hat k + 3\hat i - 6\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex} = 3\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A unit vector parallel to the vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} is
{tex}\hat d = \frac{{\vec d}}{{\left| {\vec d} \right|}} = \frac{{3\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {9 + 9 + 4} }}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{3\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k}}{{\sqrt {22} }}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{3}{{\sqrt {22} }}\hat i - \frac{3}{{\sqrt {22} }}\hat j + \frac{2}{{\sqrt {22} }}\hat k{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the points A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2) and C (11, 3, 7) are collinear, and find the ratio in which B divides AC.
question_html: Show that the points A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2) and C (11, 3, 7) are collinear, and find the ratio in which B divides AC.
solution_text: A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2), C (11, 3, 7) {tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = 1\hat i - 2\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 5\hat i - 0\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 11\hat i + 3\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex} {tex}= 4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} {/tex} {tex} = \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = 3\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \frac{3}{2}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} Thus {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} ||\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex} and one point B is common therefore A, B, C are collinear and B divides AC in 2:3.
solution_html:
A (1, -2, -8), B (5, 0, -2), C (11, 3, 7)

{tex}\overrightarrow {OA} = 1\hat i - 2\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {OB} = 5\hat i - 0\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {OC} = 11\hat i + 3\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {AB} = \overrightarrow {OB} - \overrightarrow {OA} {/tex}

{tex}= 4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \overrightarrow {OC} - \overrightarrow {OB} {/tex}

{tex} = \left( {6\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 3\left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{3}{2}\left( {4\hat i + 2\hat j + 6\hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex}\overrightarrow {BC} = \frac{3}{2}\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex}

Thus {tex}\overrightarrow {BC} ||\overrightarrow {AB} {/tex}  and one point B is common therefore A, B, C are collinear and B divides AC in 2:3.

question_text: Find the position vector of a point R which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are {tex}\left( {2\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( {\vec a - 3\vec b} \right){/tex} externally in the ratio 1 : 2. Also, show that P is the mid point of the line segment RQ.
question_html: Find the position vector of a point R which divides the line joining two points P and Q whose position vectors are {tex}\left( {2\vec a + \vec b} \right){/tex}and{tex}\left( {\vec a - 3\vec b} \right){/tex} externally in the ratio 1 : 2. Also, show that P is the mid point of the line segment RQ.

options.explanation: Let position vector of point R be {tex}\overrightarrow r {/tex} . As point R divides externally the line segment PQ in the ratio 1:2 .therefore , {tex} \overrightarrow r = \frac{{1\left( {\overrightarrow a - 3\overrightarrow b } \right) - 2\left( {2\overrightarrow a + \overrightarrow b } \right)}}{{1 - 2}} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\left( { - 3\overrightarrow a - 5\overrightarrow b } \right)}}{{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow r = 3\overrightarrow a + 5\overrightarrow b \\ {/tex} Also , mid point of the line segment RQ is : {tex}= \frac{{3\overrightarrow a + 5\overrightarrow b + \overrightarrow a - 3\overrightarrow b }}{2} = \frac{{4\overrightarrow a + 2\overrightarrow b }}{2} = 2\overrightarrow a + \overrightarrow b{/tex} ,w hich is the position vector of point P.Therefore , P is the mid point of line segment RQ.

question_text: The two adjacent sides of a parallelogram are {tex}2\hat i - 4\hat j + 5\hat k\;{/tex} and {tex}\hat i - 2\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} . Find the unit vector parallel to its diagonal. Also, find its area.
question_html: The two adjacent sides of a parallelogram are {tex}2\hat i - 4\hat j + 5\hat k\;{/tex} and {tex}\hat i - 2\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}. Find the unit vector parallel to its diagonal. Also, find its area.

options.explanation: We have, Diagonal AC as, {tex}\vec{AC}=(\hat i - 2\hat j -3\hat k )+(2\hat i-4\hat j +5\hat k)=3\hat i-6\hat j+2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{AC}|=\sqrt{(3)^2+(-6)^2+(2)^2}=\sqrt{49}=7{/tex} A unit vector parallel to {tex}\vec AC{/tex} is given by: {tex}\frac{\vec{AC}}{|\vec {AC}|}=\frac{3\hat i -6\hat j+2\hat k}{7}= \frac{3}{7}\hat i-\frac{6}{7}\hat j+\frac{2}{7}\hat k{/tex} Also, area of parallelogram {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec{AB}\times\vec{AD}|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 4}&5 \\ 1&{ - 2}&{ - 3} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} = {tex}|22\hat i +11 \hat j|{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{22^2+11^2}=\sqrt{605}=11\sqrt5{/tex}

question_text: Show that the direction cosines of a vector equally inclined to the axes OX, OY and OZ are {tex}\pm \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
question_html: Show that the direction cosines of a vector equally inclined to the axes OX, OY and OZ are {tex}\pm \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
solution_text: Let the required vector be inclined to OX, OY and OZ at angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} . Then the direction cosine of the vectors are cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} , cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} and cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} . We have, Cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + Cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + Cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 [Sum of the squares of direction cosines is 1] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 Cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} Hence, the direction cosines of the required vector which are equally inclined to the axis are {tex}\pm \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let the required vector be inclined to OX, OY and OZ at angle {tex}\alpha{/tex}.
Then the direction cosine of the vectors are cos {tex}\alpha{/tex}, cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} and cos {tex}\alpha{/tex}.
We have, Cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + Cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + Cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1       [Sum of the squares of direction cosines is 1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 Cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} 
Hence, the direction cosines of the required vector which are equally inclined to the axis are {tex}\pm \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}

Find a vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} , and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} .
question_html: Let {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}, {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex}. Find a vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}, and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} We know that the cross-product of two vectors, {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} is a vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} Hence, vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is also perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex} where {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} or some other scalar. Therefore, {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \vec i&\vec j&\vec k \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \lambda \left[ {\hat i\left( {28 + 4} \right) - \hat j\left( {7 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \vec d = 32\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j - 14\lambda \hat k{/tex} ...(i) Now given {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex} {tex}= 2\left( {32\lambda } \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - \lambda } \right) + 4\left( { - 14\lambda } \right) = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 64\lambda + \lambda - 56\lambda = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 15{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} Putting {tex}\lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get {tex}\vec d = \frac{5}{3}\left[ {32\hat i - \hat j - 14\hat k} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec d = \frac{1}{3}\left[ {160\hat i - 5\hat j - 70\hat k} \right]{/tex}

Given: Vectors {tex}\vec a = \hat i + 4\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i - 2\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}
We know that the cross-product of two vectors, {tex}\vec a \times \vec b{/tex} is a vector perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}
Hence, vector {tex}\vec d{/tex} which is also perpendicular to both {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} is {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left( {\vec a \times \vec b} \right){/tex} where {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} or some other scalar.
Therefore, {tex}\vec d = \lambda \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \vec i&\vec j&\vec k \\ 1&4&2 \\ 3&{ - 2}&7 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \lambda \left[ {\hat i\left( {28 + 4} \right) - \hat j\left( {7 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 2 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \vec d = 32\lambda \hat i - \lambda \hat j - 14\lambda \hat k{/tex}...(i)
Now given {tex}\vec c = 2\hat i - \hat j + 4\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}
{tex}\vec c.\vec d = 15{/tex}
{tex}= 2\left( {32\lambda } \right) + \left( { - 1} \right)\left( { - \lambda } \right) + 4\left( { - 14\lambda } \right) = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 64\lambda + \lambda - 56\lambda = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9\lambda = 15{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{15}}{9}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex}
Putting {tex}\lambda = \frac{5}{3}{/tex} in eq. (i), we get
{tex}\vec d = \frac{5}{3}\left[ {32\hat i - \hat j - 14\hat k} \right]{/tex}

question_html: The scalar product of the vector {tex}\hat i + \hat j + \hat k{/tex} with a unit vector along the sum of vectors {tex}2\hat i + 4\hat j - 5\hat k\;{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} is equal to one. Find the value of{tex}\;\lambda {/tex}.

options.explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a}=\widehat{i}+\widehat{j}+\widehat{k},\overrightarrow{b}=2\widehat{i}+4\widehat{j}-5\widehat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{c}=\lambda \widehat{i}+2\widehat{j}+3\widehat{k},{/tex} {tex}\vec b +\vec c =(\lambda+2)\hat i +6\hat j-2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow |\vec b+\vec c|=\sqrt{(\lambda+2)^2+40}{/tex} Therefore, a unit vector along {tex}\overrightarrow{b}+\overrightarrow{c}{/tex} is given by: {tex}\frac{\vec b +\vec c}{|\vec b+\vec c|}=\frac{(\lambda+2)\hat i +6 \hat j-2\hat k}{\sqrt{(\lambda+2)^2+40}}=1{/tex} Also, scalar product of {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} with above unit vector is 1. {tex}\therefore(\hat i+ \hat j+\hat k).\frac{(\lambda+2)\hat i +6 \hat j-2\hat k}{\sqrt{(\lambda+2)^2+40}}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow (\lambda+6)^2=(\lambda+2)^2+40\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}

question_text: If {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes, show that the vector {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=15{/tex} is equally inclined to {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} .
question_html: If {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} are mutually perpendicular vectors of equal magnitudes, show that the vector {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=15{/tex} is equally inclined to {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: vectors {tex}\vec a, \vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} are mutually perpendicular to each other and are of equal magnitude. {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=0{/tex} And {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|{/tex} Let the vector {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} be inclined to {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} at angles {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} respectively. Then, we have {tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b .} \vec{a}+\vec{c . \vec{a}})}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(|\vec{a}|^{2}+0+0\right)}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}=\frac{|\vec{a}|}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}{/tex} ......(i) {tex}\cos \beta=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b})}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\left(0+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}+0\right)}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|}=\frac{|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}|}{/tex} ......(ii) {tex}\cos \gamma=\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{c})}{\vec{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(0+0+|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}\right)}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}=\frac{|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}|}{/tex} .....(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii) As {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|{/tex} Hence, {tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\beta=\gamma{/tex} Hence, the vector {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} are equally inclined to {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: vectors {tex}\vec a, \vec b{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} are mutually perpendicular to each other and are of equal magnitude.
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{c}}=\vec{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}=0{/tex} 
And {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|{/tex} 
Let the vector {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} be inclined to {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex} at angles {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} respectively. 
Then, we have
{tex}\cos \alpha=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{a}}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}+\vec{b .} \vec{a}+\vec{c . \vec{a}})}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\left(|\vec{a}|^{2}+0+0\right)}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}=\frac{|\vec{a}|}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}|}{/tex} ......(i)
{tex}\cos \beta=\frac{(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}) \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{b}|}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{b})}{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{b}|}{/tex} 
= {tex} \frac{\left(0+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}+0\right)}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|}=\frac{|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}|}{/tex} ......(ii)
 {tex}\cos \gamma=\frac{(\vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|} {/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{c})}{\vec{|\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}| \cdot|\vec{a}|}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\left(0+0+|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}\right)}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}| \cdot|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|}=\frac{|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|}{|\vec{\mathrm{a}+\vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{c}}}|}{/tex}  .....(iii)
From (i), (ii) and (iii)
As {tex}|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|=|\vec{\mathrm{c}}|{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\beta=\gamma{/tex} 
Hence, the vector {tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}{/tex} are equally inclined to {tex}\vec{a}, \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec c{/tex}

solution_text: Given: {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b})=|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} To prove: vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are mutually perpendicular to each other. {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} {tex}\because(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b})=|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}})=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}){/tex} scalar product is commutative. {tex}\Rightarrow\left(|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}+2 \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}\right)=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(2 . \vec{\mathrm{a}} . \vec{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} Hence, {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are mutually perpendicular to each other. as {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \neq 0{/tex} is given.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b})=|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} 
To prove: vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are mutually perpendicular to each other.
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} 
{tex}\because(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}+\vec{b})=|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow(\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{a}}+\vec{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}})=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}){/tex} scalar product is commutative.
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}+2 \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}\right)=|\vec{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}+|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow(2 . \vec{\mathrm{a}} . \vec{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \vec{\mathrm{b}}=0{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} are mutually perpendicular to each other. as {tex}\vec{a} \neq 0{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \neq 0{/tex} is given.
